---
lecture: "Outliers II"
format: revealjs
metadata-files: 
  - _metadata.yml
---

{{< include _titleslide.qmd >}}

## Attribution
<br>

- Some examples and references are from [*Challenges of cellwise outliers*, by Raymaekers and Rousseeuw](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452306224000078) 

- [*Robust Statistics*, by Maronna, Martin, Yohai, Salibian-Barrera](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/book/10.1002/9781119214656)

<br>

*In memory of my mentor and friend*

Professor Ruben Zamar (1949-2023)

who contributed greatly to Robust Statistics and beyond ...

<br><br>


## Review {.scrollable}

- An **outlier** is an observation that deviates from the bulk of the data, an atypical observation. 

- As with missing values, outliers require careful diagnosis and appropriate handling before analysis.

- We defined both casewise and cellwise outliers

![](img/glass.png)
- We used simulations to examine 2 univariate estimators: the **mean** and the **median**

## Univariate estimators

**Data**: n = 180 archeological glass spectra with d = 750 wavelengths ([Detecting Deviating Data Cells](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/figure/10.1080/00401706.2017.1340909?scroll=top&needAccess=true), Rousseeuw & Van Den Bossche, *Technometrics* 2018)

Looking at the values of the wavelength V169:

```{r echo=FALSE}
library(cellWise)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(robustbase)

library(RobStatTM)

data(data_glass)
df <- data.frame(x = data_glass[,"V169"])

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "grey80", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = mean(x), color = "Mean"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = median(x), color = "Median"), linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(Mean = "red", Median = "blue")) +
  labs(title = "Wavelet Glass Spectra",
       x = "Coefficient value",
       y = "Count",
       color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Robustness *vs* Efficiency
<br>

![](img/rob-eff.png)
<br>

### Can we achieve robustness without loss of efficiency?


## Which distribution generated the data? 

```{r echo=FALSE}
set.seed(1)

# True distribution
mu_true <- 2
sigma <- 1
n <- 30

x <- rnorm(n, mu_true, sigma)

# Candidate means
mus <- c(-1.0, 0, 2.0, 3.0,5.0)

grid <- expand.grid(
  x = seq(mu_true - 4*sigma, mu_true + 4*sigma, length.out = 400),
  mu = mus
) |>
  mutate(d = dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = sigma))

dat <- tibble(x = x)

ggplot() +
  
  # Normal densities
  geom_line(
    data = grid,
    aes(x = x, y = d, color = factor(mu)),
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  
  # data points under axis
  geom_point(
    data = dat,
    aes(x = x, y = -0.02),
    size = 2.5
  ) +
  
  # vertical lines at candidate means
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = mus,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "grey40"
  ) +
  
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2", name = expression(mu)) +
  
  labs(
    x = "x",
    y = "density",
    title = ""
  ) +
  
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.05, max(grid$d))) +
  
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.line = element_line(color = "black")
  )
```

Suppose that we know which distribution generated the data but we don't know it's center, how can we estimated?

## Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) {.scrollable}

- MLE chooses the center $\mu$ that makes the observed data most probable under the assumed distribution

- The Normal distribution is given by:

$$
f(x;\mu,\sigma)
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}
\exp\!\left(
-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right)
$$

- If we assume that the data is Normally distributed, with $\sigma = 1$ but $\mu$ unknown, and with some math, we can show that


$$
\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}}
=
\arg\min_{\mu}
\sum_{i=1}^n
\!(x_i-\mu)^2
$$

## The MLE

$$
\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}}
=
\arg\min_{\mu}
\sum_{i=1}^n
\!(x_i-\mu)^2
$$

::: {.callout-note title="Definition"}
$\arg\min_{\mu}$ denotes the value of $\mu$ that minimizes the sum of squared deviations.
:::

Using Calculus, it is easy to show that 

$$\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{x}$$

#### when the data is Normal, the MLE is the sample mean

Similarly, we can show that 

#### when the data is double exponential, the MLE is the sample median

## 

But in general, we don't know the distribution. It may even be a mixture of distributions that generated contaminated data!

```{r echo=FALSE}
set.seed(1)

# Mixture parameters
w1 <- 0.9
w2 <- 0.1
mu1 <- 2
mu2 <- 5
sigma <- 1
n <- 60

# Sample from mixture
z <- rbinom(n, 1, w2)
x <- rnorm(n, mu1, sigma)
x[z == 1] <- rnorm(sum(z == 1), mu2, sigma)
dat <- tibble(x = x)

# Density grid
grid <- tibble(x = seq(-1, 8, length.out = 600)) |>
  mutate(
    comp1 = w1 * dnorm(x, mu1, sigma),
    comp2 = w2 * dnorm(x, mu2, sigma),
    mix   = comp1 + comp2
  )

grid_long <- grid |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(mix, comp1, comp2),
               names_to = "curve",
               values_to = "density") |>
  mutate(curve = factor(
    curve,
    levels = c("mix", "comp1", "comp2"),
    labels = c("Mixture", "0.9 × N(2,1)", "0.1 × N(5,1)")
  ))

ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = grid_long,
    aes(x = x, y = density, color = curve, linewidth = curve)
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Mixture" = "black",
      "0.9 × N(2,1)" = "#0072B2",  # blue
      "0.1 × N(5,1)" = "#D55E00"   # vermillion
    )
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_manual(
    values = c("Mixture" = 1.3, "0.9 × N(2,1)" = 1, "0.1 × N(5,1)" = 1)
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = dat,
    aes(x = x, y = -0.01),
    size = 2.5,
    color = "black"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.03, max(grid$mix))) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "density") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.line = element_line(color = "black"),
    legend.title = element_blank()
  )
```

## The M-estimators


$$
\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}}
=
\arg\min_{\mu}
\sum_{i=1}^n
\!(x_i-\mu)^2
$$
From the MLE to the M-estimator:

$$
\hat{\mu}_M
=
\arg\min_{\mu}
\sum_{i=1}^n
\rho(x_i-\mu)
$$

#### Instead of of squaring deviations, we measure them with a differentiable function $\rho$

## The $\rho$ function

<br>

| Robustness *vs* Efficiency|  | $\rho(r)$  | | Estimator | 
|---|--|---|--|---|
| ![](img/mean.png){width=200} | |square | | Mean |
| ![](img/median.png){width=200} | |absolute | |Median |
| &nbsp;<br> ![](img/m-estimators.png){width=200} | | &nbsp;<br><br>  differenciable? | | &nbsp;<br><br>  M-estimator |

## 

Different $M$-estimators of location have been proposed:

```{r}
# grid
x <- seq(-6, 6, length.out = 400)

df <- tibble(
  x = x,
  Mean     = 0.5 * x^2,
  Median   = abs(x),
  Huber    = Mpsi(x, psi = "huber",    cc = 1.5,   deriv = -1),
  Bisquare = Mpsi(x, psi = "bisquare", cc = 4.685, deriv = -1)) |>
  pivot_longer(-x, names_to = "type", values_to = "rho") |>
  mutate(type = factor(type, levels = c("Mean", "Huber", "Median", "Bisquare"))
  )

cc_lines <- tibble(
  type = c("Huber", "Huber", "Bisquare", "Bisquare"),
  xint = c(-1.5, 1.5, -4.685, 4.685)) |>
  mutate(type = factor(type, levels = levels(df$type)))

rho_plot <- ggplot(df, aes(x, rho)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(
    data = cc_lines,
    aes(xintercept = xint),
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ type, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "x", y = expression(rho(x))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

##

```{r echo=FALSE}
rho_plot
```

## Tuning constants

- The Huber M-estimator: quadratic near 0 and linear in the tails

- The tuning constant \(c\) marks the change and controls the robustness–efficiency trade-off.

#### Table shows values V, such that `Var(est) = V / n`

| c | Estimator | Efficiency | $\varepsilon=0$ | $\varepsilon=0.1$ |
|------:|-----------:|--------|------------------:|--------------------:|
| 0 | Median | 0.64 | 1.571 | 1.897 |
| 1.0 | Huber M | 0.90 | 1.107 | 1.443 |
| **1.345** | **Huber M** | **0.95** | **1.047** | **1.439** |
| 2.0 | Huber M | 0.99 | 1.010 | 1.549 |
| $\infty$ | Mean | 1 | 1.000 | 10.900 |

## For $\sigma$ unknown

In practice, $\sigma$ (SD of data, aka scale) is usually unknown and must be estimated from the data.

- The SD is efficient but not robust to outliers 

- The MAD (median absolute deviation) is highly robust:

$$1.4826 \, \text{median}(|x_i - \text{median}|)$$ 

- We can define an M-estimator for the scale (M-scale)

- We can also estimate location and scale jointly via M-estimation

## In R

- `mad(x)` computes the MAD

- `locScaleM(x)` in `RobStatTM` computes Huber and bisquare M-estimators (jointly for location and scale) at various asymptiotic efficiency values

## Simulation design

- Clean data: Generate a sample of size 100 from a Normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1

- Contaminated data: Generate a sample of size 100, with 90% from $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ and 10% from $\mathcal{N}(0,10)$

- Compute the mean and the median

- Repeat 10000 times

- Approximate the variance of the estimators


##

```{r}
set.seed(200)

rcont_norm <- function(n, eps = 0.10, mu0 = 0, sigma0 = 1, sigma1 = 10) {
  x <- rnorm(n, mean = mu0, sd = sigma0)
  
  k <- ceiling(eps * n)              
  if (k > 0) {
    idx <- sample(1:n, k)
    x[idx] <- rnorm(k, mean = mu0, sd = sigma1)
  }
  x
}

#no contamination

eps = 0
sim <- replicate(1000, {
    x <- rcont_norm(100, eps = eps)
    c(
      Mean     = mean(x),
      Median   = median(x),
      Bisquare_3.44 = locScaleM(x, psi="bisquare", eff=0.85)$mu,
      Bisquare_4.68 = locScaleM(x, psi="bisquare", eff=0.95)$mu
    )
  })

  
var0  <- apply(t(sim), 2, var)

#with contamination
eps = 0.20
sim <- replicate(1000, {
    x <- rcont_norm(100, eps = eps)
    c(
      Mean     = mean(x),
      Median   = median(x),
      Bisquare_3.44 = locScaleM(x, psi="bisquare", eff=0.85)$mu,
      Bisquare_4.68 = locScaleM(x, psi="bisquare", eff=0.95)$mu
    )
  })
  
var20 <- apply(t(sim), 2, var)

```

## Approximated Variance of the Bisquare M-estimator

<br>

```{r echo = FALSE}
library(knitr)

eff <- c(
  Mean = 1,
  Median = 0.64,
  Bisquare_3.44 = 0.85,
  Bisquare_4.68 = 0.95
)

table_var <- tibble(
  Estimator = names(var0),
  Eff = unname(eff[Estimator]),
  `ε = 0` = unname(var0),
  `ε = 0.20` = unname(var20)
) |>
  mutate(
    Eff = ifelse(is.na(Eff), "", sprintf("%.2f", Eff)),
    across(c(`ε = 0`, `ε = 0.20`), ~ round(.x, 3))
  )

kable(table_var, align = "lccc",
      col.names = c("Estimator", "Eff", "ε = 0", "ε = 0.20"))
```

## Detecting Outliers

How do we know if the data contains outliers?

<br>

#### The **3-$\sigma$ rule**

- Classical rule: observations far from the mean are considered outliers

- What is "far"? We can measure

$$z_i = \frac{x_i-\bar x}{s}$$

- Flag as outlier if: $|z_i| > 3$

## iClicker 1: interpretation of the rule

<br>

With the 3-$\sigma$ rule:

$$|z_i| = \left|\frac{x_i-\bar x}{s}\right| > 3 $$

an observation is flagged as an outlier if


- A. The observation is more than 3 standard deviations from the mean

- B. The observation is in the largest 3% of the data

- C. The observation is larger than 3 times the mean

- D. The observation is more than 3 units from the median

## iClicker 2: use the rule
<br>

Which points are flagged as outliers using the 3-$\sigma$ rule?

```{r}
x <- c(-2,-1,0,1,2,13,20)

mean(x); sd(x)

z  <- (x-mean(x))/sd(x)
z
```

- A. Both $x=13$ and $x=20$ are flagged

- B. Only $x=20$ is flagged

- C. All points are flagged

- D. None of points are flagged

## Masking
<br>

- The mean and SD are sensitive to outliers

- Extreme observations pull the mean toward them and inflate the standard deviation, reducing their standardized distance

- Robust alternative: use median and MAD (or other robust loc-scale estimators)

#### Robust z-score:

$$z_i^{rob} = \frac{x_i-\text{median}}{\text{MAD}}$$

**Rule**: $|z_i^{rob}| > 3$

## iClicker 3: use the robust rule

<br>

Which points are flagged as outliers using the 3-$\sigma$ rule?

```{r}
x <- c(-2,-1,0,1,2,13,20)

median(x); mad(x)

z  <- (x-median(x))/mad(x)
z
```

- A. Both $x=13$ and $x=20$ are flagged

- B. Only $x=20$ is flagged

- C. All points are flagged

- D. None of points are flagged

## Key Takeaways

<br>

- Classical estimators (mean, SD) are highly sensitive to outliers

- Robust estimators reduce the influence of extreme observations

- M-estimators provide a flexible compromise between efficiency and robustness

- The tuning constant controls this trade-off

- Robust methods improve both estimation and outlier detection
